topic: A person discovers their child has the same teacher they had
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a physical blow. A simple notification, yet it sent a tremor through me that settled deep in my chest. My son, Leo, was in the third grade at Elmwood Elementary. And the teacher? Mrs. Albright. The woman whose relentless enthusiasm and saccharine praise I had spent the last five years trying to ignore. I stared at the screen, my jaw tightening until my molars ached. It was impossible. A cruel cosmic joke, orchestrated by some unseen, malicious hand. I felt a cold, sharp anger bloom in my stomach, quickly morphing into a simmering, tightly controlled fury. I didn't call the school. I didn't want to hear the explanation. I just wanted to burn the notification, erase the impossible connection.

[story 2]

I watched Leo meticulously draw a picture of a rocket ship during dinner, his brow furrowed in concentration. Then, the inevitable happened. He looked up, his eyes bright with a familiar, eager spark, and whispered, "Mrs. Albright said we need to practice our letters again tomorrow." The air in the dining room seemed to instantly thin. My hand, mid-reach for the salt shaker, froze above the table. A tight knot formed in my throat, a sensation like swallowing gravel. Every muscle in my shoulders tensed, pulling my spine rigid. I forced a neutral expression onto my face, the kind I usually reserved for difficult phone calls. *No. Absolutely not.* The thought echoed with a sharp, undeniable resentment. I wanted to scream at the universe for delivering this unwelcome coincidence.

[story 3]

The glossy pamphlet arrived on my doorstep, a welcome-to-school brochure from Elmwood. The photo on the cover was unmistakable: Mrs. Albright, beaming, surrounded by smiling children. My breath hitched. I picked up the brochure, the paper feeling flimsy and insulting in my grip. My knuckles went white as I unfolded it, the sound of the crinkling paper amplifying the drumming pulse in my ears. A wave of intense displeasure washed over me, hot and prickly. I tossed the pamphlet onto the nearest surface—the kitchen counter—with unnecessary force. I needed distance. I needed to look away from that image, from that shared history that suddenly felt deeply, personally offensive.
